feed.title: Combined summary - BIP 3 and issues on BIPs

entry.title: Combined summary - BIP 3 and issues on BIPs
entry.summary: The ongoing discussion among Bitcoin Improvement Proposal (BIP) contributors highlights the complexity and challenges involved in managing feedback across the development community. The debate focuses on the efficiency of incorporating feedback directly into the BIPs repository as opposed to relying solely on external platforms like mailing lists, blogs, or social media for such discussions. There is a suggestion to classify feedback into three main categories: acknowledged concerns by BIP authors that remain unresolved, reasonable concerns broadly acknowledged but disagreed upon by BIP authors, and feedback generally dismissed as irrelevant or trolling. A significant point raised involves the practicality of adding an "Unresolved Issues" section to BIPs for the first category of feedback, which could help track concerns that are recognized but not yet addressed. For the second category, it's proposed that feedback could be documented under a specific BIP managed by editors, even if the original authors do not agree with the criticisms. This approach aims to maintain a structured record of dissenting opinions and ensure they are accessible for review without diluting the original intent of the authors. Critics of current practices argue that the lack of a centralized, authoritative space for such discussions may lead to scatter and inefficiency in addressing concerns that can affect the implementation and acceptance of BIPs. The removal of the comment system from the BIPs due to low engagement and quality issues has further exacerbated this problem, with some advocating for a more structured approach through pull requests and curated summaries of discussions. This method, however, raises concerns about increased workload for BIP editors and potential underrepresentation of community viewpoints. Moreover, the conversation touches on the historical handling of feedback, particularly noting the changes implemented with BIP3 which removed links to empty wikis while preserving those with substantive content. There's an acknowledgment that more could be done to disseminate changes and enhance participation from BIP authors in documenting both resolved and open issues to foster clearer understanding and collaboration within the community. Ultimately, the dialogue underscores a need for a balance between authorial ownership of BIPs and community involvement in refining and challenging proposals, aiming for a documentation process that is both inclusive and efficient. The proposed solutions, while not perfect, reflect a concerted effort towards improving how feedback is integrated and represented within the BIP ecosystem. The link to BIP3 can be found [here](https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/blob/master/bip-0003.mdcomments).
